q: What distinguishes an inhibitory postsynaptic potential (IPSP) from an excitatory postsynaptic potential (EPSP)?
options: An EPSP hyperpolarizes the postsynaptic membrane; an IPSP depolarizes it
An IPSP hyperpolarizes the membrane (often via Cl- influx or K+ efflux), making it less likely to fire; an EPSP depolarizes it, increasing firing likelihood
Only EPSPs involve ligand-gated ion channels; IPSPs use voltage-gated channels
IPSPs are produced only at neuromuscular junctions; EPSPs occur only in the brain
explanation: Inhibitory neurotransmitters (e.g., GABA) cause Cl- influx or K+ efflux, hyperpolarizing the membrane and moving it further from threshold, reducing the chance of an action potential.
